edges.description: CBS deficiency disrupts the transsulfuration branch of methionine metabolism.
edges.source: Methionine-Cycle and Sulfur Amino Acid Disease Group

edges.description: cblC disease disrupts intracellular cobalamin processing for both remethylation and mutase pathways.
edges.source: Methionine-Cycle and Sulfur Amino Acid Disease Group
edges.target: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: MTHFR deficiency disrupts folate-dependent homocysteine remethylation.
edges.source: Methionine-Cycle and Sulfur Amino Acid Disease Group

edges.description: MAT1A deficiency disrupts hepatic methionine-to-S-adenosylmethionine flux.
edges.source: Methionine-Cycle and Sulfur Amino Acid Disease Group

edges.description: The upstream biochemical consequence of impaired CBS-dependent cystathionine synthesis is homocysteine accumulation.
Persistent homocysteine burden and deficient transsulfuration drive thromboembolism, ectopia lentis, skeletal abnormalities, and learning difficulties.
Homocysteine accumulation in CBS deficiency contributes to thromboembolic disease.
Severe CBS deficiency includes ocular connective-tissue involvement with ectopia lentis.
Severe CBS deficiency can include neurodevelopmental learning difficulties.
Severe CBS deficiency includes skeletal abnormalities as part of the homocystinuria phenotype.
The combined remethylation and mutase cofactor defect causes high homocysteine, low methionine, and high methylmalonic acid.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure
edges.target: Hyperhomocysteinemia with Hypomethioninemia and Methylmalonic Acidemia

edges.description: Late-onset cblC disease can involve neuropathy, encephalopathy, psychiatric disease, seizures, kidney disease, thrombotic microangiopathy, and pulmonary hypertension.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: cblC remethylation failure contributes to elevated homocysteine.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Reduced cobalamin-dependent remethylation lowers methionine production in cblC disease.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Failed adenosylcobalamin support for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase raises methylmalonic acid in cblC disease.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Combined methylmalonic acidemia and homocystinuria in late-onset cblC disease can manifest as neuropathy or myelopathy.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Late-onset cblC disease can progress to cognitive decline.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Adult cblC disease can present with psychiatric disease including psychosis.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Late-onset cblC disease can include renal involvement.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: Late-onset cblC disease can include pulmonary hypertension with heart failure.

edges.description: Late-onset cblC disease can involve seizures.
edges.source: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

edges.description: The remethylation block increases plasma homocysteine.
Higher persistent methionine concentrations are associated with demyelination and neurologic abnormalities.
edges.target: Severe Persistent Hypermethioninemia and White Matter Injury

edges.description: MAT I/III deficiency causes persistent isolated methionine elevation.
Severe persistent hypermethioninemia in MAT I/III deficiency can cause cerebral white-matter injury.
MAT I/III deficiency can include delayed language development as part of its neurologic spectrum.

edges.description: Elevated total homocysteine reports impaired CBS-dependent transsulfuration in classical homocystinuria.

Elevated homocysteine is one component of the combined cblC biochemical pattern.

edges.description: Elevated blood methionine reports impaired MAT I/III-dependent methionine-to-S-adenosylmethionine flux.
Decreased methionine reports impaired remethylation in cblC disease.

nodes.description: Severe persistent MAT I/III deficiency-associated hypermethioninemia can injure cerebral white matter.

evidence.snippet: Methionine adenosyltransferase I/III deficiency is not just a benign disease. Severe persistent hypermethioninemia can cause brain injuries, especially in the white matter.

nodes.description: Skeletal abnormalities are part of the severe childhood CBS-deficiency phenotype.

evidence.snippet: Severely affected patients usually present in childhood with ectopia lentis, learning difficulties and skeletal abnormalities.

nodes.description: Betaine is used to lower homocysteine by promoting alternate remethylation to methionine and is used in CBS deficiency and cobalamin/remethylation disorders.

evidence.snippet: Betaine is used to lower homocysteine as a substrate for betaine‐homocysteine methyltransferase, which can convert homocysteine into methionine.

nodes.description: CBS deficiency impairs conversion of homocysteine into cystathionine in the methionine catabolic pathway, producing homocysteine accumulation and multisystem disease dominated by vascular, ocular, skeletal, and neurologic complications.

evidence.snippet: Cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS) deficiency is a rare inherited disorder in the methionine catabolic pathway, in which the impaired synthesis of cystathionine leads to accumulation of homocysteine.
Pyridoxine responsiveness appears to be the key factor determining the clinical course of HCU.

nodes.description: Renal disease is a recognized late-onset cblC manifestation.

evidence.snippet: Individuals with late-onset cblC may be asymptomatic until manifesting neuropsychiatric symptoms, thromboembolic events, and renal disease.

nodes.description: MMACHC-related cblC disease impairs intracellular cobalamin processing, reducing methylcobalamin support for methionine synthase and adenosylcobalamin support for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. This produces the combined biochemical pattern of hyperhomocysteinemia, methylmalonic acidemia, and hypomethioninemia.
nodes.id: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

evidence.snippet: Cobalamin C (cblC) deficiency is the most common inborn error of intracellular cobalamin metabolism caused by pathogenic variant(s) in MMACHC and manifests with methylmalonic acidemia, hyperhomocysteinemia, and hypomethioninemia with a variable age of presentation.

nodes.description: MAT I/III deficiency can include delayed language development.

evidence.snippet: Of these 15 patients, 10 demonstrated neurological abnormalities, with delayed language development, learning difficulties or abnormal brain imaging findings.

nodes.description: Severely affected childhood-onset CBS deficiency commonly includes lens dislocation.

evidence.snippet: Severely affected patients usually present in childhood with ectopia lentis, learning difficulties and skeletal abnormalities.

nodes.description: Folic acid is used as an adjunct in cblC treatment regimens with hydroxocobalamin and betaine to support remethylation-focused metabolic correction.
nodes.id: Folic acid adjunct therapy for cblC disease

evidence.snippet: A regimen of high-dose hydroxocobalamin (25 mg/day) together with betaine and folic acid resulted in rapid and sustainable biochemical correction, resolution of psychosis, improvement of neurological functions, and amelioration of brain and spinal cord lesions.

nodes.description: Parenteral hydroxocobalamin is central to treatment of cblC disease and is often combined with betaine and folate-based adjuncts.

evidence.snippet: A regimen of high-dose hydroxocobalamin (25 mg/day) together with betaine and folic acid resulted in rapid and sustainable biochemical correction, resolution of psychosis, improvement of neurological functions, and amelioration of brain and spinal cord lesions.
Patients with this mutation tend to have a relatively better response to hydroxocobalamin, better metabolic control, and more favorable neurological outcomes.

nodes.id: Hyperhomocysteinemia with Hypomethioninemia and Methylmalonic Acidemia

nodes.description: Elevated homocysteine occurs in CBS deficiency, MTHFR deficiency, and cblC disease.

evidence.snippet: Cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS) deficiency is a rare inherited disorder in the methionine catabolic pathway, in which the impaired synthesis of cystathionine leads to accumulation of homocysteine.

nodes.description: MAT I/III deficiency can produce severe persistent elevation of blood methionine.

evidence.snippet: Patients with demyelination showed significantly higher blood methionine concentrations at baseline (1102 vs. 396 µmol/L), and their blood methionine remained markedly elevated despite a low-methionine diet.

nodes.description: cblC remethylation failure can lower methionine production.

evidence.snippet: The disease is characterised by an elevation of methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine and a decreased production of methionine.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is a potential option for severe refractory MAT I/III deficiency because hepatic methionine adenosyltransferase activity is the affected pathway entry point, but evidence remains limited.
nodes.id: Liver transplantation for refractory MAT I/III deficiency

evidence.snippet: Liver transplantation may be a potential treatment option for refractory methionine adenosyltransferase I/III deficiency.

nodes.description: Pyridoxine non-responders and partial responders with CBS deficiency often require dietary methionine restriction, commonly with other homocysteine- lowering interventions.

evidence.snippet: Severely affected patients usually present during childhood with learning difficulties, ectopia lentis and skeletal abnormalities; they are pyridoxine non-responders (NR) or partial responders (PR) and require treatment with a low-methionine diet and/or betaine.

nodes.description: Methionine restriction is used for MAT I/III deficiency with severe hypermethioninemia, though persistent marked methionine elevation can remain despite diet in patients with demyelination.
nodes.id: Low-methionine diet for severe MAT I/III deficiency

evidence.snippet: Patients with demyelination showed significantly higher blood methionine concentrations at baseline (1102 vs. 396 µmol/L), and their blood methionine remained markedly elevated despite a low-methionine diet.

nodes.description: MAT1A deficiency reduces hepatic methionine adenosyltransferase I/III activity, impairing S-adenosylmethionine synthesis and causing persistent hypermethioninemia. Severe persistent hypermethioninemia is associated with demyelination and brain white-matter injury.

evidence.snippet: Methionine adenosyltransferase I/III deficiency is not just a benign disease. Severe persistent hypermethioninemia can cause brain injuries, especially in the white matter.
Methionine adenosyltransferase I/III deficiency is not just a benign disease. Severe persistent hypermethioninemia can cause brain injuries, especially in the white matter.

Cobalamin C (cblC) deficiency is the most common inborn error of intracellular cobalamin metabolism caused by pathogenic variant(s) in MMACHC and manifests with methylmalonic acidemia, hyperhomocysteinemia, and hypomethioninemia with a variable age of presentation.

nodes.description: Severe MTHFR deficiency is a folate-metabolism disorder that blocks methionine-cycle remethylation, causing elevated plasma homocysteine and homocystinuria.

evidence.snippet: Severe methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase deficiency is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder of folate metabolism causing elevated plasma homocysteine levels and homocystinuria (MIM 236250).
biallelic pathogenic variants in MTHFR identified by molecular genetic testing.

nodes.description: Cognitive decline can become a prominent progressive symptom in late-onset cblC disease.

evidence.snippet: Besides the initial symptoms, the disease progressed in most patients and cognitive decline became the most frequent symptom overall.

Patients with demyelination showed significantly higher blood methionine concentrations at baseline (1102 vs. 396 µmol/L), and their blood methionine remained markedly elevated despite a low-methionine diet.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased methionine reports impaired remethylation in cblC disease.
readouts.target: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

nodes.description: This MONDO class represents a genetically heterogeneous pathway group whose component disorders affect transsulfuration, remethylation, intracellular cobalamin processing, and S-adenosylmethionine synthesis.
nodes.id: Methionine-Cycle and Sulfur Amino Acid Disease Group

evidence.snippet: Utilizing a combined multiple-tier algorithm, we demonstrate that NBS and specialized metabolic care result in substantial benefits for individuals with MTHFR deficiency, CBS deficiency, neonatal cbl deficiency, and to some extent, cbl-responsive MMA and cblC deficiency.
The disease is characterised by an elevation of methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine and a decreased production of methionine.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated methylmalonic acid reports impaired adenosylcobalamin-dependent methylmalonyl-CoA handling in cblC disease.
readouts.target: Cobalamin-Dependent Remethylation and Mutase Cofactor Failure

nodes.description: Combined cobalamin-processing defects produce methylmalonic acid elevation.

evidence.snippet: The disease is characterised by an elevation of methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine and a decreased production of methionine.

nodes.description: Late-onset cblC disease frequently includes neuropathy or myelopathy.

evidence.snippet: The most frequent clinical symptoms were neuropathy/myelopathy, encephalopathy, psychiatric symptoms, thrombotic microangiopathy, seizures, kidney disease, mild to severe pulmonary hypertension with heart failure and thrombotic phenomena.

nodes.description: Late-onset cblC disease can present with severe psychiatric disturbance including psychosis.

evidence.snippet: A regimen of high-dose hydroxocobalamin (25 mg/day) together with betaine and folic acid resulted in rapid and sustainable biochemical correction, resolution of psychosis, improvement of neurological functions, and amelioration of brain and spinal cord lesions.

nodes.description: Late-onset cblC disease can include pulmonary hypertension with heart failure.

evidence.snippet: The most frequent clinical symptoms were neuropathy/myelopathy, encephalopathy, psychiatric symptoms, thrombotic microangiopathy, seizures, kidney disease, mild to severe pulmonary hypertension with heart failure and thrombotic phenomena.

nodes.description: Pyridoxine supplementation is used for CBS-deficient homocystinuria when residual enzyme responsiveness is present and can substantially improve biochemical control.

evidence.snippet: Pyridoxine-responsive patients generally achieved tHcy concentrations below 50 μmol/L

nodes.description: Seizures occur in the late-onset cblC disease spectrum.

evidence.snippet: The most frequent clinical symptoms were neuropathy/myelopathy, encephalopathy, psychiatric symptoms, thrombotic microangiopathy, seizures, kidney disease, mild to severe pulmonary hypertension with heart failure and thrombotic phenomena.

nodes.id: Severe Persistent Hypermethioninemia and White Matter Injury

nodes.description: Learning difficulties are part of the severe childhood CBS-deficiency phenotype.

evidence.snippet: Severely affected patients usually present in childhood with ectopia lentis, learning difficulties and skeletal abnormalities.

nodes.description: Mild or late-presenting CBS deficiency can present with thromboembolism.

evidence.snippet: Cystathionine β-synthase (CBS) deficiency (classical homocystinuria) has a wide range of severity. Mildly affected patients typically present as adults with thromboembolism and respond to treatment with pyridoxine.
Cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS) deficiency is a rare inherited disorder in the methionine catabolic pathway, in which the impaired synthesis of cystathionine leads to accumulation of homocysteine.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated total homocysteine reports impaired CBS-dependent transsulfuration in classical homocystinuria.
Elevated plasma homocysteine reports the folate-remethylation block in severe MTHFR deficiency.
Elevated homocysteine is one component of the combined cblC biochemical pattern.

nodes.description: A recurrent MMACHC variant associated with milder or later-onset cblC disease and more favorable hydroxocobalamin response in a large Chinese cohort.

evidence.snippet: Patients with this mutation tend to have a relatively better response to hydroxocobalamin, better metabolic control, and more favorable neurological outcomes.
